sentences.text: The bit-manipulation (bitmanip) extension collection is comprised of several component extensions to the base RISC-V architecture that are intended to provide some combination of code-size reduction, performance improvement, and energy reduction.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: While the instructions are intended for general use, some instructions are more useful in certain domains than in others.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: Hence, several smaller bitmanip extensions are provided.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: Each of these smaller extensions is grouped by common function and use case, and each has its own Zb*-extension name.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: Each bitmanip extension includes a group of several bitmanip instructions that have similar purposes and can often share the same logic.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: Some instructions are available in only one extension, while others are available in several.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: The instructions have mnemonics and encodings that are independent of the extensions in which they appear.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: Thus, when implementing extensions with overlapping instructions, there is no redundancy in logic or encoding.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: The bitmanip extensions are defined for RV32 and RV64.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: The bitmanip extension follows the convention in RV64 that w-suffixed instructions (without a dot before the w) ignore the upper 32 bits of their inputs, operate on the least-significant 32 bits as signed values, and produce a 32-bit signed result that is sign-extended to XLEN.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: Bitmanip instructions with the suffix .uw have one operand that is an unsigned 32-bit value that is extracted from the least-significant 32 bits of the specified register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: Other than that, these perform full-XLEN operations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: Bitmanip instructions with the suffixes .b, .h, and .w only look at the least-significant 8 bits, 16 bits, and 32 bits of the input (respectively) and produce an XLEN-wide result that is sign-extended or zero-extended, based on the specific instruction.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: The bit-manipulation instructions comprise the following extensions:
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: Zba: * Zbb: * Zbs: * Zbc: * Zbkb: * Zbkc: * Zbkx:
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

sentences.text: Below is a list of all of the instructions that are included in these extensions, along with their specific mapping:

sentences.text: The B standard extension comprises instructions provided by the Zba, Zbb, and Zbs extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > "B" Extension for Bit Manipulation, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: [zba,reftext=Address generation instructions]
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > "B" Extension for Bit Manipulation, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: The Zba instructions can be used to accelerate the generation of addresses that index into arrays of basic types (halfword, word, doubleword) using both unsigned word-sized and XLEN-sized indices: a shifted index is added to a base address.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zba: Extension for Address generation, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: The shift and add instructions do a left shift of 1, 2, or 3 because these are commonly found in real-world code and because they can be implemented with a minimal amount of additional hardware beyond that of the simple adder.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zba: Extension for Address generation, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: This avoids lengthening the critical path in implementations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zba: Extension for Address generation, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: While the shift and add instructions are limited to a maximum left shift of 3, the slli instruction (from the base ISA) can be used to perform similar shifts for indexing into arrays of wider elements.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zba: Extension for Address generation, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: The slli.uw -- added in this extension -- can be used when the index is to be interpreted as an unsigned word.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zba: Extension for Address generation, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: These instructions perform the sign extension or zero extension of the least-significant 8 bits or 16 bits of the source register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbb: Extension for Basic bit-manipulation, Version 1.0.0 > Sign extension and zero extension

sentences.text: These instructions replace the generalized idioms `slli rd,rs,(XLEN-<size>) + srai` (for sign extension of 8-bit and 16-bit quantities) and `slli + srli` (for zero extension of 16-bit quantities).

sentences.text: Bitwise rotation instructions are similar to the shift-logical operations from the base spec.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbb: Extension for Basic bit-manipulation, Version 1.0.0 > Bitwise rotation

sentences.text: However, where the shift-logical instructions shift in zeros, the rotate instructions shift in the bits that were shifted out of the other side of the value.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbb: Extension for Basic bit-manipulation, Version 1.0.0 > Bitwise rotation

sentences.text: Such operations are also referred to as ‘circular shifts’.

sentences.text: *orc.b* sets the bits of each byte in the result rd to all zeros if no bit within the respective byte of rs is set, or to all ones if any bit within the respective byte of rs is set.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbb: Extension for Basic bit-manipulation, Version 1.0.0 > OR Combine

sentences.text: One use-case is string-processing functions, such as *strlen* and *strcpy*, which can use *orc.b* to test for the terminating zero byte by counting the set bits in leading non-zero bytes in a word.

sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbkc: Extension for Carry-less multiplication for Cryptography, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: These are the crucial instructions needed to efficiently implement the GHASH operation, a critical operation in some cryptographic workloads such as the AES-GCM authenticated encryption scheme.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbkc: Extension for Carry-less multiplication for Cryptography, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: See Zbc for further instruction details for these two instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbkc: Extension for Carry-less multiplication for Cryptography, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: These instructions implement a "lookup table" for 4 and 8 bit elements inside the general purpose registers. rs1 is used as a vector of N-bit words, and rs2 as a vector of N-bit indices into rs1.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbkx: Extension for Crossbar permutations, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: Elements in rs1 are replaced by the indexed element in rs2, or zero if the index into rs2 is out of bounds.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbkx: Extension for Crossbar permutations, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: These instructions are useful for expressing N-bit to N-bit boolean operations, and implementing cryptographic code with secret dependent memory accesses (particularly SBoxes) such that the execution latency does not depend on the (secret) data being operated on.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbkx: Extension for Crossbar permutations, Version 1.0.0

sentences.text: Description:: The pack instruction packs the XLEN/2-bit lower halves of rs1 and rs2 into rd, with rs1 in the lower half and rs2 in the upper half.

sentences.text: Encoding (RV32):: [wavedrom, , svg] .... {reg:[ { bits: 7, name: 0x13, attr: ['OP-IMM'] }, { bits: 5, name: 'rd' }, { bits: 3, name: 0x5 }, { bits: 5, name: 'rs' }, { bits: 12, name: 0x698 } ]} ....
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rev8

sentences.text: Encoding (RV64):: [wavedrom, , svg] .... {reg:[ { bits: 7, name: 0x13, attr: ['OP-IMM'] }, { bits: 5, name: 'rd' }, { bits: 3, name: 0x5 }, { bits: 5, name: 'rs' }, { bits: 12, name: 0x6b8 } ]} ....
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rev8

sentences.text: Description:: This instruction reverses the order of the bytes in rs.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rev8

sentences.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- let input = X(rs); let output : xlenbits = 0; let j = xlen - 1;
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rev8

sentences.text: foreach (i from 0 to (xlen - 8) by 8) { output[i..(i + 7)] = input[(j - 7)..j]; j = j - 8; }
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rev8

sentences.text: .Note [NOTE, caption="A" ] The *rev8* mnemonic corresponds to different instruction encodings in RV32 and RV64.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rev8

sentences.text: .Software Hint [NOTE, caption="SH" ] The byte-reverse operation is only available for the full register width.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rev8

sentences.text: To emulate word-sized and halfword-sized byte-reversal, perform a `rev8 rd,rs` followed by a `srai rd,rd,K`, where K is XLEN-32 and XLEN-16, respectively.
sentences.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rev8

sentences.text: The result is each element in rs2 replaced by the indexed element in rs1, or zero if the index into rs2 is out of bounds.
